title: Transaction Management, Locking & Distributed Transactions
description: Transaction management is the backbone of enterprise data consistency in SAP systems. ABAP's transaction model is unique — it combines SAP Logical Unit of Work (LUW) with database LUW, enqueue-based locking, update work processes for async changes, and RFC-level transaction integrity. This guide covers the full stack from SAP LUW fundamentals to distributed saga patterns for Staff+ architects.

sections.title: SAP LUW vs Database LUW

description.value:
The SAP Logical Unit of Work (LUW) is a fundamental concept that distinguishes ABAP from other enterprise platforms. Unlike a database LUW, which is bounded by database commits, the SAP LUW spans multiple database LUWs within a single business transaction. A user action (screen save) triggers business logic that may involve multiple database operations, but from the user's perspective, the entire unit either succeeds or fails atomically.

Dual-LUW Model: ABAP manages two LUWs simultaneously: (1) The database LUW — each individual database operation (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) is implicitly committed when the database executes any Open SQL statement with implicit commit behavior (DDL, RFC calls, etc.). (2) The SAP LUW — bracketed by the first screen call and the COMMIT WORK / ROLLBACK WORK statement. Between these boundaries, all database changes are held in the update task or the database's transaction context.

Why ABAP needs this: In a traditional web application, one HTTP request = one transaction. In SAP, a single user interaction may span multiple screens, function modules, RFC calls, and update tasks. The SAP LUW ensures that a user pressing 'Save' either commits all changes or rolls back everything, even if the underlying process involves 20 database operations, 3 RFC calls, and an update work process execution.

sections.codeBlock:
FUNCTION z_bapi_create_salesorder.
*"----------------------------------------------------------------------
*" BAPI for creating sales order with LUW control
*"----------------------------------------------------------------------
  CALL FUNCTION 'Z_SD_SALESORDER_CREATE'
    EXPORTING
      iv_sales_order_data = ls_order_data
    IMPORTING
      ev_sales_order_id   = lv_sales_order_id
    EXCEPTIONS
      error               = 1.

  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
    ROLLBACK WORK.
    MESSAGE e001(zsd) INTO lv_msg.
    RETURN.
  ENDIF.

  CALL FUNCTION 'Z_SD_UPDATE_INVENTORY'
    EXPORTING
      iv_order_id = lv_sales_order_id
    TABLES
      tt_materials = lt_materials.

  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
    ROLLBACK WORK.
    MESSAGE e002(zsd) INTO lv_msg.
    RETURN.
  ENDIF.

  COMMIT WORK.
  MESSAGE s003(zsd) WITH lv_sales_order_id.
ENDFUNCTION.

* Key points:
* - SAP LUW: Between first function call and COMMIT WORK
* - Multiple DB LUWs may occur internally
* - ROLLBACK WORK on any failure reverts all changes
* - COMMIT WORK makes all changes permanent

rows: Scope
Spans user interaction (multiple screens, function modules, RFCs)
Single database statement or implicit commit boundary
Boundary
First screen call → COMMIT WORK / ROLLBACK WORK
Each individual SQL statement or explicit COMMIT
Atomicity
All-or-nothing for the entire business transaction
Within a single database transaction
Persistence
COMMIT WORK persists all changes in the SAP LUW
DB COMMIT persists the database transaction
Failure handling
ROLLBACK WORK reverts all changes since LUW start
Partial rollback possible via savepoints
Visibility
Changes visible to other SAP LUWs only after COMMIT WORK
Changes visible to other DB sessions only after DB COMMIT
Nesting
SAP LUW cannot be nested (one per work process)
Database LUW can be nested via savepoints
comparisonTable.takeaway: The SAP LUW is the business transaction boundary. The database LUW is the technical execution boundary. ABAP manages both, ensuring business-level atomicity regardless of database behavior.

sections.title: Locking in ABAP (Enqueue)

description.value:
ABAP locking uses an enqueue server — a separate work process that maintains a lock table in shared memory. When you call ENQUEUE_* (generated function modules based on lock objects in SE11), the request is sent to the enqueue server, which checks if the lock can be granted. If another user holds a conflicting lock, your request is either rejected (immediate response) or queued (if ENQUEUE with WAIT is used).

Lock Granularity: Lock objects in ABAP can be defined on multiple table levels — header + item, for example. The enqueue server manages locks at the key-level (specific record) or table-level (entire table). Lock arguments are passed as parameters to the ENQUEUE function module, and the lock is registered against the exact data key.

Lock Modes: (1) S (Shared) — multiple users can read the same record. No exclusive access needed. (2) E (Exclusive) — one user holds write access. Other E and X locks are rejected. S locks are still allowed. (3) X (eXclusive, no cumulative) — the strongest lock. Only one user can hold it. No other locks, not even S, are allowed.

Lock Lifetime: By default, locks are released at COMMIT WORK (the SAP LUW boundary). This means locks are held throughout the entire user transaction — from screen to screen. This is appropriate for most scenarios but can lead to lock contention. For long-running dialogs, consider scope-based locking or optimistic locking with timestamp checks.

sections.codeBlock:
CALL FUNCTION 'ENQUEUE_EZ_SALESORDER'
  EXPORTING
    mode_zsalesorder       = 'E'
    mandt                  = sy-mandt
    salesorder            = lv_salesorder_id
    _scope                = '3'
    _wait                 = abap_true
    _collect              = abap_true
  EXCEPTIONS
    foreign_lock          = 1
    system_failure        = 2.

IF sy-subrc = 1.
* Lock held by another user — show their info
  MESSAGE e401(zsd) WITH lv_salesorder_id
                     lv_enqueue_user
                     INTO lv_msg.
ELSEIF sy-subrc = 2.
* Enqueue server unavailable — fallback to DB lock
  CALL FUNCTION 'Z_DB_LOCK_SALESORDER'
    EXPORTING
      iv_order_id = lv_salesorder_id.
ENDIF.

* --- Business logic ---

CALL FUNCTION 'DEQUEUE_EZ_SALESORDER'
  EXPORTING
    mode_zsalesorder = 'E'
    mandt           = sy-mandt
    salesorder      = lv_salesorder_id.

* Scope parameter:
* _scope = '1' → lock survives until COMMIT WORK
* _scope = '2' → lock released at end of LUW (default)
* _scope = '3' → lock survives until explicit DEQUEUE

decisionTree.prompt: Which lock mode should I use for this business operation?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Does this operation read data, modify data, or both?" Read-only needs S (Shared) lock. Exclusive modification needs E (Exclusive) lock. Critical exclusive access needs X lock.

branches.condition: Read-only access — user views data, no modification
branches.path: S (Shared) lock. Multiple readers allowed. Use for display-only transactions. If reading for potential edit, consider E lock to prevent mid-read changes.

branches.condition: Exclusive modification — user edits and saves
branches.path: E (Exclusive) lock. Single writer. Other E locks rejected, but S locks still allowed. Use for most edit scenarios (sales order change, material master update).

branches.condition: Critical system operation — no concurrent access tolerated
branches.path: X (eXclusive, non-cumulative) lock. Blocks all other locks including S. Use for mass updates, data migrations, batch jobs. High contention risk — minimize hold duration.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: E is your default for write operations. X is for system-critical exclusive access — document why S or E won't suffice. Remember locks auto-release at COMMIT WORK. For long-running screens, use _scope='2' with manual DEQUEUE after save.

sections.title: Update Work Process & V1/V2

description.value:
The update work process is ABAP's mechanism for asynchronous database updates. When your program calls COMMIT WORK, any function modules started with IN UPDATE TASK are executed in dedicated update work processes. This decouples the user-facing dialog from potentially slow database operations — the user gets immediate feedback while the database is updated asynchronously.

V1 (Critical) vs V2 (Non-Critical): SAP categorizes updates into two priorities. V1 updates execute first, sequentially, in the order they were scheduled. If a V1 update fails, the entire update request is rolled back — the V2 update never executes, and the user can see the error in SM13. V2 updates are non-critical — they execute after V1 succeeds and are independent of each other. If a V2 fails, it is logged but the V1 changes remain committed.

Failure Handling: The update module runs in a separate work process. If the update function module encounters a fatal error, the system increments a counter. After a configurable number of retry attempts (update retries in RZ10), the update request is placed in error status in SM13. The original dialog user sees the error, or an administrator must manually re-process the failed update.

Staff+ Note: The V1/V2 distinction is an early example of priority-based task scheduling — a concept now central to modern event-driven architectures and CQRS. V1 is the command side (write path), V2 is the event side (reaction). Understanding this pattern shows architectural maturity.

sections.codeBlock:
FUNCTION z_update_salesorder_inventory.
*"----------------------------------------------------------------------
*" V1 Update — Critical inventory adjustment
*"----------------------------------------------------------------------
*" Called via: CALL FUNCTION 'Z_UPDATE_INVENTORY' IN UPDATE TASK
  UPDATE zproduct SET
    quantity = quantity - lv_quantity
    WHERE product_id = lv_product_id.

  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
* Critical failure — abort entire update request
    MESSAGE e010(zsd) WITH lv_product_id INTO lv_dummy.
* V1 failure automatically suppresses V2.
  ENDIF.
ENDFUNCTION.

FUNCTION z_update_material_docs.
*"----------------------------------------------------------------------
*" V2 Update — Non-critical document generation
*"----------------------------------------------------------------------
  CALL FUNCTION 'Z_CREATE_DOCUMENTS'
    EXPORTING
      iv_order_id = lv_order_id
    EXCEPTIONS
      error       = 1.

  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
    MESSAGE w011(zsd) WITH lv_order_id INTO lv_dummy.
*   V2 failure is logged — V1 changes remain committed.
*   Admin reviews SM13 and re-processes V2 manually.
  ENDIF.
ENDFUNCTION.

* Scheduling the updates:
CALL FUNCTION 'Z_UPDATE_INVENTORY'
  IN UPDATE TASK
  EXPORTING
    lv_product_id = ls_item-product_id
    lv_quantity   = ls_item-quantity.

CALL FUNCTION 'Z_CREATE_DOCUMENTS'
  IN UPDATE TASK
  EXPORTING
    lv_order_id   = lv_order_id.

COMMIT WORK.
* At COMMIT WORK:
* 1. V1 (Z_UPDATE_INVENTORY) executes in update work process
* 2. If V1 succeeds, V2 (Z_CREATE_DOCUMENTS) executes
* 3. If V1 fails, V2 never runs — ROLLBACK entire update

quantifiedTradeoff.title: V1 vs V2 Update — Synchronous vs Asynchronous Cost Analysis

v1_critical.monthlyPenalty: $2,800 — 500K × 150ms × 730h = 60.8 core-hours on update work processes. All V1 changes must succeed for update request to commit.

v2_non_critical.monthlyPenalty: $1,200 — 1,500 failed/day × 30 min reconciliation = 45 admin-hours/month. Plus downstream data inconsistency risk.

calculation.breakeven: If V2 failure rate exceeds 2%, move critical derivation logic to V1. The V2 failure handling cost (admin time + data inconsistency) exceeds the benefit of async decoupling at >2% failure rate.

sections.title: Open SQL Transaction Control

description.value:
Open SQL in ABAP provides transaction control statements that operate within the SAP LUW framework. While the SAP LUW is the primary transaction unit, Open SQL offers granular control for specific scenarios: database LUW boundaries, savepoints, and implicit commit triggers.

Implicit Commits: Certain Open SQL statements implicitly terminate the current database LUW: DDL statements (CREATE TABLE, DROP TABLE), transactional RFC calls (CALL FUNCTION ... IN BACKGROUND TASK), and database-specific operations. Understanding these implicit commits is critical — they can silently commit partial work, violating the SAP LUW contract.

COMMIT WORK vs COMMIT CONNECTION: The standard COMMIT WORK ends the SAP LUW. COMMIT CONNECTION (for secondary database connections) commits only the secondary connection, not the primary LUW. This is crucial for multi-database transactions in system landscapes.

SQL Rollback Areas: ABAP supports database savepoints via COMMIT WORK with AND WAIT and implicit rollback areas at the database level. For complex operations spanning multiple database statements, consider using explicit database savepoints (if supported by the DBMS) to enable partial rollback within the SAP LUW.

sections.codeBlock:
DATA: lv_order_id TYPE vbeln.

* Every DB operation starts a new database LUW implicitly
INSERT INTO zsalesorder VALUES ls_order.

IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  ROLLBACK WORK.
  EXIT.
ENDIF.

* Update related table
UPDATE zproduct SET quantity = quantity - ls_item-quantity
  WHERE product_id = ls_item-product_id.

IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  ROLLBACK WORK.   " Reverses both INSERT and UPDATE
  EXIT.
ENDIF.

* Explicit COMMIT ends the SAP LUW
COMMIT WORK.

* ---
* Isolation level control:
EXEC SQL.
  SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE
ENDEXEC.

* Safe pattern: Use COMMIT WORK only after all operations succeed.
* Avoid implicit commits (DDL, RFC) mid-transaction —
* they silently commit the current database LUW.
* Check sy-subrc after every operation before COMMIT.

decisionTree.prompt: Should I use COMMIT WORK explicitly or rely on implicit commit behavior?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Is this operation part of a multi-step business transaction?" Yes → explicit COMMIT WORK at the end. No → consider whether implicit commit is acceptable.

branches.condition: Multi-step business operation (create order + update inventory + log)
branches.path: Explicit COMMIT WORK at the end. All-or-nothing atomicity. Use ROLLBACK WORK if any step fails. Never rely on implicit commit for multi-step operations.

branches.condition: Single-step data modification with no rollback risk
branches.path: Explicit COMMIT WORK still preferred for clarity. Implicit commit on RFC call or DDL statement creates hidden coupling — the commit happens when you least expect it.
No COMMIT WORK needed. Database reads don't participate in transaction boundaries. Use COMMIT WORK only if you need to refresh database cursors or release locks.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: Always use explicit COMMIT WORK. Implicit commits are a common source of partial commits in production incidents. CI checks should flag function modules with DDL or RFC calls inside a transaction without explicit COMMIT/ROLLBACK.

sections.title: RFC Transactional Integrity (tRFC/qRFC)

description.value:
Transactional RFC (tRFC) and Queued RFC (qRFC) extend the SAP LUW across system boundaries. When you call CALL FUNCTION ... IN BACKGROUND TASK, the RFC request is not sent immediately — it is saved to the database (SMQ1/SMQ2 tables) as part of the current SAP LUW. Only when COMMIT WORK is executed does the tRFC request become visible and begin processing in the target system.

Idempotency via TID: Each tRFC call is assigned a unique transaction ID (TID). The target system stores processed TIDs. If a network failure occurs and the tRFC is retried, the target system checks the TID — if it was already processed, the call is skipped (duplicate detection). This guarantees exactly-once (or at-least-once with deduplication) semantics.

LUW Preservation: tRFC participates in the SAP LUW. If you schedule two tRFC calls and then issue ROLLBACK WORK, both tRFC requests are removed from the queue — they never execute. If COMMIT WORK succeeds, both requests become active in the target system. This provides ACID-like guarantees across systems.

qRFC: Queued RFC adds ordering guarantees. All tRFC calls with the same queue name are processed sequentially in the target system. This is essential for scenarios where message ordering matters (e.g., processing sales order items in sequence). The queue acts as a FIFO channel, ensuring causal ordering.

sections.codeBlock:
DATA: lv_order_id TYPE vbeln.

* Schedule tRFC calls — they participate in the current SAP LUW
CALL FUNCTION 'Z_CREATE_ORDER_IN_ERP'
  IN BACKGROUND TASK
  DESTINATION 'ERP_SYSTEM'
  EXPORTING
    iv_order_data = ls_order_data.

CALL FUNCTION 'Z_UPDATE_CRM'
  IN BACKGROUND TASK
  DESTINATION 'CRM_SYSTEM'
  EXPORTING
    iv_order_id   = lv_order_id.

* Both tRFC requests are stored in SMQ1
* Only after COMMIT WORK do they start executing

COMMIT WORK.

* If ROLLBACK WORK is issued before COMMIT, both tRFC
* requests are removed from the queue — they never execute.

* ---
* qRFC with queue name for ordered processing:
CALL FUNCTION 'Z_PROCESS_ORDER_ITEMS'
  IN BACKGROUND TASK
  DESTINATION 'ERP_SYSTEM'
  QUEUE 'ORDER_ITEMS'
  EXPORTING
    it_items = lt_items.

* qRFC guarantees FIFO processing per queue name
* Monitor: SMQ1 (outbound), SMQ2 (inbound)
* SM58: tRFC error log

adversarialResponse.challenge: "tRFC is too complex for our use case. We're just sending a notification. Why not use plain RFC or HTTP call?"
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "tRFC is the standard SAP way. Never use plain RFC for cross-system updates." (dogmatic, no context)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "You're right that tRFC adds complexity. Let me break down when the overhead is justified. For a notification that can be lost or duplicated without business impact (like 'order submitted' email), a plain RFC or async HTTP call is simpler and faster — you don't need the TID deduplication, LUW integration, or retry mechanism. However, for any operation where missed or duplicate execution has business cost (like 'inventory reduced' or 'invoice created'), tRFC's exactly-once semantics are not overhead — they're the minimum viable reliability layer. Here's my rule: if the receiving side has side effects (DB writes, external API calls), use tRFC. If it's purely informational (logging, notification, cache eviction), use plain RFC. For your notification case, let's use asynchronous RFC (aRFC) — it's fire-and-forget without the tRFC overhead."
adversarialResponse.pattern: nuanced-tradeoff — validate the concern, provide a decision framework (side-effect vs informational), and offer a simpler alternative (aRFC) where appropriate.

sections.title: Distributed Transactions in SAP

description.value:
Distributed transactions in SAP span multiple resource managers — databases, message queues, external systems. The standard mechanism is the XA protocol (X/Open XA), which uses a two-phase commit (2PC) protocol to ensure atomicity across all participants. SAP's transaction manager coordinates the prepare and commit phases, ensuring that either all participants commit or all roll back.

Two-Phase Commit: Phase 1 (prepare) — the coordinator asks each participant if they can commit. Each participant writes their changes to durable storage (WAL) and responds 'ready'. Phase 2 (commit) — if all participants respond 'ready', the coordinator tells them to commit. If any participant responds 'no' or times out, the coordinator tells all participants to roll back.

The Saga Pattern Alternative: For long-running distributed transactions (spanning seconds or minutes), 2PC is impractical — holding locks for minutes degrades throughput. The saga pattern decomposes the distributed transaction into a sequence of local transactions, each with a compensating transaction. If a step fails, the saga runs the compensating transactions in reverse order. SAP implementations use BAPI + tRFC with compensating BAPIs for saga-style distributed transactions.

Staff+ Perspective: The choice between XA and Saga is a tradeoff between consistency guarantees and throughput. XA provides ACID guarantees but requires all participants to be available during the transaction. Saga provides eventual consistency with better availability. In SAP landscapes, use XA for short-lived, high-value transactions (payment posting) and Saga for long-running, multi-step processes (order-to-cash).

sections.codeBlock:
* XA Transaction Boundary (via transaction manager)
* In SAP, XA is typically managed by the SAP CCMS or TPM

* Phase 1: Prepare
CALL FUNCTION 'Z_PREPARE_DB_UPDATE'
  EXPORTING
    iv_transaction_id = lv_xid.

CALL FUNCTION 'Z_PREPARE_QUEUE_UPDATE'
  EXPORTING
    iv_transaction_id = lv_xid.

* Phase 2: Commit (if both prepared successfully)
IF lv_all_prepared = abap_true.
  CALL FUNCTION 'Z_COMMIT_DB_UPDATE'
    EXPORTING
      iv_transaction_id = lv_xid.
  CALL FUNCTION 'Z_COMMIT_QUEUE_UPDATE'
    EXPORTING
      iv_transaction_id = lv_xid.
ELSE.
  ROLLBACK WORK.
ENDIF.

* ---
* Saga Pattern (Compensating Transactions):

* Step 1: Create order (forward transaction)
CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_SALESORDER_CREATEFROMDAT2'
  EXPORTING
    salesdocumentin = lv_order_id.

* Step 2: Reserve inventory (forward)
CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_MATERIAL_RESERVE'
  EXPORTING
    material = ls_item-material.

* If Step 2 fails, compensate Step 1:
CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_SALESORDER_CHANGE'
  EXPORTING
    salesdocument = lv_order_id
  TABLES
    return        = lt_return.
" Set deletion flag = 'X' to cancel the order

* Saga ensures: Order created → reserved, OR order cancelled
* No global lock held during the multi-step process

quantifiedTradeoff.title: XA (2PC) vs Saga — Consistency vs Throughput

xa_2pc.monthlyPenalty: $4,500 — 1M × 500ms = 500K seconds. 5% of transactions encounter lock contention. Each contention adds 2s retry. Worse: if any participant is down, 2PC blocks entirely.

saga.monthlyPenalty: $8,000 — 50K transactions × 30s = 1.5M seconds. 2% require compensation. Each compensation takes 10s. No global locks means no contention, but higher latency.

calculation.breakeven: At >3 participants with >50ms round-trip latency each, XA's lock contention cost exceeds Saga's compensation cost. For high-volume (1M+ daily) with <500ms total, XA wins. For multi-step business processes with user interaction, Saga is the only viable pattern.

sections.title: RAP Transactional Composition

description.value:
RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) redefines transaction management for the Fiori era. Instead of manual ENQUEUE/DEQUEUE and COMMIT WORK calls, RAP provides a declarative transaction model where the framework manages locking, LUW boundaries, and database updates automatically.

EML (Entity Manipulation Language): EML is the RAP language for modifying business objects. A single MODIFY ENTITIES statement can create, update, and delete multiple business objects in one call. The framework ensures all modifications are executed within a single SAP LUW — if any operation fails, the entire change set is rolled back.

Managed vs Unmanaged: In the managed scenario, RAP automatically handles locking (via the enqueue server), deterministic sequencing (operation order), and commit/rollback. The developer only writes the business logic in behavior definition methods. In unmanaged scenarios, the developer explicitly controls save and clean up phases using the standard RAP interaction phases: early, modify, save.

Transactional Composition: RAP business objects can be composed using associations. When you modify the root entity, the framework ensures that all child entities in the composition tree participate in the same LUW. This replaces the classic pattern of manually scheduling multiple update tasks and coordinating their execution.

sections.codeBlock:
* Managed RAP scenario — transaction handled by framework
MODIFY ENTITIES OF ZR_SalesOrder
  ENTITY SalesOrder
    UPDATE FIELDS ( NetAmount CurrencyCode )
    WITH VALUE #( ( %key-SalesOrderID = lv_order_id
                    NetAmount       = lv_new_amount
                    CurrencyCode    = 'USD' ) )
  ENTITY SalesOrderItem
    UPDATE FIELDS ( Quantity )
    WITH VALUE #( ( %key-SalesOrderID = lv_order_id
                    %key-ItemID       = lv_item_id
                    Quantity          = lv_new_qty ) )
  FAILED   DATA(ls_failed)
  REPORTED DATA(ls_reported).

IF ls_failed IS NOT INITIAL.
* Framework automatically rolls back the entire change set
  ROLLBACK WORK.
  EXIT.
ENDIF.

* Unmanaged scenario — developer controls transaction:
METHOD save.
  LOOP AT items INTO DATA(ls_item).
    CALL FUNCTION 'Z_UPDATE_INVENTORY'
      EXPORTING
        iv_product_id = ls_item-product_id
        iv_quantity   = ls_item-quantity.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

METHOD cleanup.
* Release locks, undo buffers
  CALL FUNCTION 'DEQUEUE_ALL'.
ENDMETHOD.

* RAP ensures: Single LUW for all MODIFY ENTITIES operations.
* Framework generates ENQUEUE_* calls automatically for managed BOs.

organizationalDimension.prompt: How should the RAP transaction design be organized across teams?
challenges: Team A owns SalesOrder behavior definition. Team B owns Inventory behavior definition. Both need transactional consistency when creating orders and adjusting inventory.
Managed vs unmanaged decision affects team autonomy — managed is faster but constrains locking strategy. Unmanaged gives full control but requires deeper transaction expertise.
Testing transactional behavior across team boundaries requires integration test environments with shared EML consumers.
organizationalDimension.teamStructure: Create a Transaction Architecture Guild with representatives from each BO-owning team. Define a Decision Record (ADR) for managed vs unmanaged per business object. Managed is default. Unmanaged requires ADR approval from the guild. Cross-BO transactions follow a choreography pattern — each team publishes events, and the consuming team manages their own LUW.
organizationalDimension.staffPlusTip: RAP transaction design is an organizational architecture problem. The BO decomposition determines the transaction boundaries. Poor decomposition = leaky transactions across team boundaries. Use bounded context mapping to align BO boundaries with team boundaries.

sections.title: Staff+ Transaction Design Patterns

description.value:
At Staff+ level, transaction design is about failure-aware system design — how to build systems that degrade gracefully under lock contention, network partitions, and partial failures. These patterns apply across SAP and non-SAP systems:

Deadlock Prevention: ABAP's enqueue server detects deadlocks by building a wait-for graph. When a deadlock is detected, one request is rolled back. To prevent deadlocks: (1) Always acquire locks in the same order across all transactions. Document the order in an ADR. (2) Use a timeout on lock waits (_WAIT = ABAP_FALSE + retry with user notification). (3) For batch jobs, acquire all locks upfront and release in reverse order.

Lock Timeout Strategy: Never use _WAIT = ABAP_TRUE with infinite timeout in dialog transactions. Use a configurable wait time (e.g., 5 seconds) and then either (a) show the lock owner to the user, (b) queue the request for async processing, or (c) switch to optimistic concurrency with version numbers.

Distributed Consistency Patterns: (1) Exactly-once semantics via tRFC TID deduplication — guarantees that a remote function call is executed exactly once even if the LUW is retried. (2) Saga with compensation table — maintain a transaction log table that tracks each saga step. If a step fails, the compensation reads from the log and reverses each completed step. (3) Idempotency key pattern — the caller generates a unique idempotency key for each request. The receiver stores processed keys and skips duplicates.

sections.codeBlock:
* Lock ordering — consistent across all transactions
* Order: Client → SalesOrder → Item → Inventory

CALL FUNCTION 'ENQUEUE_EZ_CLIENT'
  EXPORTING
    client_id = lv_client_id.

CALL FUNCTION 'ENQUEUE_EZ_SALESORDER'
  EXPORTING
    salesorder_id = lv_order_id.

* ---
* Lock timeout handling with retry logic
CONSTANTS: lc_max_retries TYPE i VALUE 3,
           lc_wait_sec    TYPE i VALUE 5.

DO lc_max_retries TIMES.
  CALL FUNCTION 'ENQUEUE_EZ_INVENTORY'
    EXPORTING
      product_id = lv_product_id
      _wait      = abap_false
    EXCEPTIONS
      foreign_lock = 1.

  IF sy-subrc = 0.
    EXIT.  " Lock acquired
  ELSEIF sy-index = lc_max_retries.
    MESSAGE e402(zsd) WITH lv_product_id INTO lv_msg.
    ROLLBACK WORK.
    RETURN.
  ENDIF.

* Backoff: wait and retry
  WAIT UP TO lc_wait_sec SECONDS.
ENDDO.

* ---
* Idempotency key pattern (for tRFC idempotency):
DATA: lv_idempotency_key TYPE string.

lv_idempotency_key = |{ lv_order_id }-{ sy-datum }-{ sy-uzeit }|.

CALL FUNCTION 'Z_PROCESS_ORDER'
  IN BACKGROUND TASK
  DESTINATION 'ERP'
  EXPORTING
    iv_idempotency_key = lv_idempotency_key
    iv_order_data      = ls_order.

* Target system stores processed keys in Z_IDEMPOTENCY_LOG
* Duplicates with same key are silently skipped

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Why are we spending 3 weeks on transaction design? The system has been working fine for 10 years without all these patterns. This is over-engineering."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "The old system is unreliable and needs to be replaced." (dismissive, no evidence)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "You're right that the existing system has been running for a decade. Let me share data that justifies the investment. Last month, our production incident log shows 47 lock contention incidents — 23 required manual intervention in SM12 to release orphaned locks. Average resolution time: 45 minutes. During peak hours, lock contention caused order processing to back up by 2 hours. Estimated monthly cost: $28,000 in admin overhead + delayed orders. The patterns I'm proposing (consistent lock ordering, configurable timeouts, idempotency keys) are not new architecture — they're systematic solutions to the same problems that caused those 47 incidents. The 3-week investment pays for itself in 2 months of incident cost avoidance. Additionally, these patterns are prerequisites for moving to S/4HANA Cloud where enqueue server behavior is different — we're derisking the cloud migration."
adversarialResponse.pattern: data-driven-justification — quantify the cost of existing instability, show ROI timeline, and connect to strategic initiatives (cloud migration) to build organizational buy-in.

details: Staff+ Insight: Transaction management in SAP is not just about COMMIT and ROLLBACK. It's about understanding the interplay between SAP LUW, database LUW, enqueue locking, update tasks, and cross-system integrity. At Staff+ level, you design transaction boundaries that align with business operations, not technical convenience. Interviewers probing transaction knowledge want to see that you understand consistency models, failure modes, and the tradeoffs between synchronous (XA/2PC) and asynchronous (Saga/Eventual) consistency patterns.
